Abstract
--------

This paper analyses the 878 Scopus-indexed articles on the global green hydrogen value chain (GHVC) published between 2009 and 2024. The analysis reveals a sharp increase in research activity from 2020 to 2024, in parallel with the rise in global greenhouse gas emissions. The highest average citation rate was 1063, observed in 2013. Leading contributors include Nanyang Technological University and the Federal University of Rio Grande do Norte. At the same time, China and Singapore emerge as the most active countries in research output, particularly in green hydrogen and renewable energy. The International Journal of Hydrogen Energy is identified as the most relevant journal, with Wang Y as the most cited author and the 2013 article by Carmo M as the most cited paper. Notably, China and India exhibit high emissions and high research productivity, indicating no clear linear correlation between emissions and research output. Keyword analysis identifies "hydrogen production" and "green hydrogen" as the most frequently used terms. Thematic analysis identifies ten major research areas: hydrogen production; hydrogen cost and economics; storage solutions; hydrogen as a fuel and energy carrier; integration with renewable energy; sustainability and environmental aspects; policies and legislations; technological advancements; infrastructure development; and market growth and commercialization. These themes are gaining increasing attention. The paper discusses the opportunities and challenges associated with mapping the current landscape and future directions of GHVC research. In conclusion, the article highlights the need to align technology and policy to advance hydrogen and calls for further research to fill knowledge gaps.

1. Introduction
---------------

The shift toward a sustainable energy future is essential to address the current climate crisis, enhance energy security, and ensure the long-term viability of energy resources. Green hydrogen (GH), with renewable sources as a production source, has emerged as a vital element in this transition [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0001)]. According to the International Renewable Energy Agency [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0002)], GH is expected to significantly impact reaching the 1.5 °C climate goal by 2050 as an energy transition component. GH 2 plays an essential and crucial role in the clean energy ecosystem by providing promising solutions to decrease carbon emissions in industry, electricity generation, and transportation networks [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0003)].

With the growing global focus on cleaner energy technologies, it is crucial to understand the complications of the green hydrogen value chain (GHVC) to guide both research and policy decisions. The GHVC refers to the comprehensive operation of producing, storing, transporting, and utilizing the hydrogen produced from renewable energy sources [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0004),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0005)]. GH production methods include obtaining hydrogen from natural gas through electrolysis, thermochemical water splitting, and carbon capture and storage (CCS). Unlike "brown" or "grey" hydrogen produced from fossil fuels, GH provides environmental benefits by reducing carbon emissions [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0005)].

[Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#fig0001) illustrates the components of the GHVC to provide a visual aid for understanding. Its basis is to produce clean electricity. Wind turbines, solar panels, and hydroelectric dams convert natural resources such as wind, sunlight, and water into electricity [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0006)] and this energy is crucial for the next step. Water is the raw material for hydrogen production, and this is supplied from stable and clean sources to ensure electrolytic quality. An electrolyser is an apparatus that uses electrical power to separate water molecules (H 2 O) into hydrogen (H 2) and oxygen (O 2) [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0001)]. This process is clean, which emits only oxygen into the atmosphere. Moreover, the surplus heat from electrolysis can be utilized for heating, enhancing the process's efficiency.

GH has versatility. It can be used directly in applications such as car fuel cells. However, it can also be converted into other clean energy sources. Nitrogen (N 2) in the air combines with hydrogen to produce green ammonia. This derivative is functional for fertilizer production and has the potential to serve as a source of oil in the future [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0007)]. Hydrogen combines with captured carbon dioxide (CO 2) to produce methanol. This synthetic fuel can be used for many applications where methanol is usually derived from fossil fuels. Hydrogen is a light fuel and requires special handling [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0008)].

It can be transported as a gas or liquid in high-pressure pipelines or trucks. Similarly, ammonia and methanol can be transported through existing pipelines, ships, and trucks [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0007)]. The applications of GH and its derivatives are diverse. These clean fuels can replace fossil fuels in many industrial processes, reducing carbon emissions. Hydrogen fuel cells could power heavy vehicles such as trucks, eliminating pollution from long-distance transportation. Clean-burning hydrogen has the potential to fuel future aircraft and ships, tackling a significant challenge in decarbonizing these industries. Green ammonia produced from GH could replace ammonia from fossil fuels in fertilizer production [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0009)]. Given its growing importance in the global energy landscape, [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#fig0001) sets the stage for understanding the significance of studying the GHVC.

It is observed that the transition to GHVC may not emerge by itself due to its immaturity and complexity [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0010)]. To comprehend the influential trends of research, different collaborations, as well as thematic areas that will support long-term GHVC benefits, need to be considered regarding hydrogen production, storage, and utilization through renewable energy sources. However, existing studies on GHVC are still fragmented. For instance, several researchers such Kar et al. [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0011)] have done a bibliometric analysis on the hydrogen economy, which focused on publication trends, citation impacts, and multidisciplinary aspects. However, this study did not specifically address the GHCV.

In addition, Raman et al. [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0012)] examined green-hydrogen research themes focusing on bibliometric indicators, thematic distributions, and clusters, but not explicitly addressing the GHVC or its comprehensive integration across sectors. Catumba et al.’s [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0013)] focuses on trends in hydrogen production research and key publications without including the broader integration challenges of the GHVC. Even though Du et al.’s [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0010)] systematic review analysed the decarbonization potential and economics of GH; it did not cover bibliometric trends and thematic gaps.

Frankowska and Blonski [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0014)] analysed influential works in hydrogen supply chains but did not focus on the GHVC and their thematic analysis. Navarro et al. [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0005)] came up with a roadmap that looked at engineering developments and cost pressures in the hydrogen value chain. Despite this, there was no bibliometric analysis of research trends or thematic gaps within the GHVC in their study. Sillero et al. [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0015)] carried out a bibliometric analysis on hydrogen production from dark fermentation regarding research progress, trends, and technological advances. However, they did not investigate wider aspects of GHVC, including its thematic gaps beyond dark fermentation. Hence, the current study addresses the following RQs to analyse research trends and explore research themes in GHVC.
*   ■RQ1: What are the annual publication trends and citation patterns in GHVC research? Which sources, affiliations, and countries have the most influence? 
*   ■RQ2: What papers and keywords are essential on GHVC at present? How do these reflect the present focus of research themes? 
*   ■RQ3: What are the significant thematic findings from this thematic analysis of GHVC literature? How do these findings reveal emerging research gaps and future directions? 

To answer these RQs, the research uses bibliometric and thematic analysis methods. Bibliometrics includes statistical analyses of publications to track and evaluate research trends by examining the year-wise publications volume, subject-wise publications, citation pattern, authors' productivity, keyword pattern, collaborative networks, etc. [[[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0016), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0018), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0019), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0020)]. On the other hand, looking at thematic analysis, it is a tool that brings out recurring themes, central concepts, and changes in studies [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017)]. While combining these techniques, the study presents an all-rounded view of research trends while tackling key breakthroughs and voids to be filled in this area. This research aims to examine publication trends and themes to provide practical observations on the progress and future opportunities of GHVC research. This study makes valuable contributions to the present state of GHVC research by exploring the key areas of the study and suggesting future research endeavours.

This research is laid out as follows: [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#sec0002) presents the study's research methods. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#sec0005) describes the trends in studies about GHVC research. [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#sec0020) draws the thematic and literature analysis of the GHVC research. [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#sec0028) consists of the discussion and implications of GHVC. Finally, [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#sec0042) addresses the conclusion, limitations, and future work of GHVC.

2. Methodology
--------------

[Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#fig0002) presents the research method, which centres on a systematic technique using precise keywords to retrieve academic papers from 2009 to 2024. Next, a thorough bibliometric assessment was conducted, encompassing performance analysis and scientific mapping, to examine different publication patterns, including trends based on year, subject, author, nation, journal, citation, and publication coupling. Next, a Sankey diagram and a comprehensive literature examination were used to examine the study progression over time, facilitating the discovery of pivotal themes. Finally, the study culminates with an exhaustive examination, implications, and conclusions derived from the results.

### 2.1. The preferred reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses (PRISMA) method

[Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#fig0003) shows the PRISMA method of GHVC research. [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#fig0003) displays the systematic procedure used to identify and select the relevant publications [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0021)] for the GHVC research. In the Identification phase, a total of 1115 articles were identified by conducting database searches from 1990 to 2024. The search used the keywords "Green hydrogen AND economy" and "Green hydrogen AND production". Articles were assessed according to specific criteria in the screening and eligibility phase.

These criteria included the period from 2009 to 2024, specific subject areas such as Environmental Science, Energy, Engineering, Chemical Engineering, Physics and Astronomy, Chemistry, Materials Science, and Mathematics, document types such as article, review, conference paper, and conference review, and English language articles. After this, 196 were removed because they did not match the specified criteria, and as a result, 888 articles were considered for eligibility. Finally, during the insertion phase, 878 papers were selected for study after eliminating 2 duplicate entries and 8 incomplete records. The [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#fig0003) provides a clear visualization of the entire process, starting from the initial identification and ending with the final inclusion of articles for the study.

### 2.2. Analysis tools

This study uses a systematic and bibliometric methodology to present a comprehensive of the literature [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0016)]. Applications of data mining and analysis processes are essential for acquiring relevant and dependable study outcomes [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017)]. The VOS viewer was used to conduct co-citation analysis [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0022)]. Additionally, co-occurrence examination of keywords [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0022)], bibliographic couplings, and co-authorship examination [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0023)] were also conducted. Content analysis was facilitated using a VoS viewer, which streamlines bibliometric mapping and network estimation [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017)]. To analyse publishing patterns, word associations, and a publication on GHVC based on affiliations were also conducted using Biblioshiny on R Studio [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0024),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0025)].

Bibliometric indicators were assessed using MS Excel. Graph mapping and explanatory analysis tools were utilized to analyse the bibliometric data on GHVC collected from the Scopus database. The co-citation technique examined the trends of citations in the scholarly works on GHVC research [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0026)]. By analysing the frequency of co-citations, this method assisted in identifying the most significant publications and writers in the subject. The co-citation analysis was conducted employing the VOS viewer software, which allows for the Graph mapping and explanatory analysis [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0016)].

Keyword co-occurrence approach [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017)] was used to explore the patterns in which keywords appear together in the literature on GHVC. This assisted in identifying the most common and emerging ideas, concepts, and trends in the subject by analysing the frequency of specific keywords appearing together in publications. This approach considers both author-provided keywords and indexed keywords [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0025)]. Bibliographic coupling [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0016)] is a method that measures the level of resemblance among papers by examining the references they have in common. Articles that reference the same set of sources were regarded as bibliographically linked. By analysing bibliographic coupling trends this study aimed to identify groups of linked articles and potential research frontiers in the GHVC domain [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0026)].

Co-authorship analysis explored the trends of cooperation among authors in the scholarly works on GHVC domain. By examining the co-authorship networks, this method assisted in identifying the most productive authors, prominent research teams, and potential research partnerships in the domain [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0017)]. The study used VOS viewer, a software tool, to perform co-citation analysis, keyword co-occurrence analysis, and bibliographic coupling. This software enables the creation of bibliometric maps and network visualizations [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#bib0024)]. The study seeks to comprehensively grasp the research methods, trends, and knowledge frameworks within the GHVC literature by utilizing various bibliometric analytic tools.

3. Bibliometric analysis
------------------------

This section provides insights and conceptual structure of publications in the field of GHVC. The section is divided into twelve subsections presenting the results and findings of “Publication trends, average annual citations, most relevant publication sources, bibliometric analysis, most relevant authors, most relevant affiliations, top countries, co-occurrence of keywords (authors’ and indexed), bibliographic couplings (sources, countries, keywords)”. Through examination and visual representation, the study provides useful observations of research trends in the field of GHVC, thereby improving the knowledge in the field.

### 3.1. Trends in GHVC publications

[Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825005301#tbl0001) was generated using R-Studio software where the Scopus database file was imported into Biblioshiny and presents a summary of publication trends in the field of GHVC from the year 2009 to 2024. The restriction was set on the research publications published during 2009 to 2024 accounting to current interest in this field of research. A growth of 27.26 % annually was observed with the data derived from 878 documents published in 338 sources (Journals and Conferences), providing a strong growing interest in the field of GHVC. Per current research scenario, the age of documents is 1.97 on an average and 32.05 citations have been recorded per document.

# Hydrogen: Element of the future

On the grounds of [Forschungszentrum Jülich](https://www.fz-juelich.de/en), a German research institute, there are office buildings and laboratories as well as large test halls. In the background, the steady hum of various systems and turbines is audible. The busy streets and wide roads are reminiscent of a city where the pulse of science can be felt from the very first second. Research is being carried out here on topics that have the potential to completely transform our society and industry over the coming years. There is a particularly sharp focus on a sustainable energy sector that aims to make the future more livable.

## Increasing efficiency for a sustainable future

The key issue with the production of hydrogen is that electrolyzers lose efficiency over time and that makes the conversion of energy into hydrogen less viable. In their research, Eva Jodat and Christine Heume are getting to the bottom of these efficiency losses in electrolyzers in order to minimize them. Here, ZEISS X-ray microscopes, such as the Xradia 620 Versa, are deployed.

Using 3D imaging methods, the researchers can inspect non-destructively the heart of the electrolyzer, the membrane electrode assembly, and detect changes. They use computed tomography (CT) to take several X-ray images from different angles and use these to reconstruct a precise three-dimensional image of the complex structures and events within the membrane.

The structural changes can then be investigated further using scanning electron microscopy, e.g. with the ZEISS GeminiSEM. This is how the researchers gain important insights for improving these electrolyzers. They make their findings available to the entire research community through publications and also pass them on directly to cooperation partners from industry.

This is reflected not only in the intensive exchange of knowledge between the stakeholders, but also in an impressive research megawatt electrolyzer, which is operated at the research site together with industrial partner Siemens Energy. Even Eva Jodat and Christine Heume are always impressed by the system, which is several meters high. The large electrolyzer enables them to conduct their research in hydrogen technology not only on a laboratory scale, but also on a scale relevant for industry.

The findings from this flagship project will then be incorporated into future generations of electrolyzers: "With the research and continued development of water electrolysis on a megawatt scale, we are setting new standards worldwide in the cooperation between science and industry," says Eva Jodat.

### Hydrogen as a contribution to the energy transition

In the past, the scientists could analyze a structural change in the membrane. These findings can be incorporated directly into the redesign of electrolyzer components and thus improve hydrogen production. In doing so, they make an important contribution to the energy transition. If they can become more efficient and cost-effective, this type of energy converter could be installed in every industrial park or residential property in future. For example, green hydrogen can be produced using wind or solar energy, which can then be used in the production of steel or plastics.

In the private sphere, hydrogen is used in the mobility sector for trucks and cars or to run personal heating system, among other things. Households can also use the electricity from their solar systems to produce hydrogen for their private use. Thanks to innovative containers and the existing gas infrastructure, hydrogen can also be stored and converted into electricity as required. As no environmentally harmful by-products are produced during the conversion into energy, their increased use makes a significant contribution to the success of the energy transition.

The opportunity to play a key role in shaping the future motivates the two scientists in their research work. Both agree that society needs to find a way out of the oil-based, unsustainable energy industry. Their research makes a valuable contribution to this goal, and they are particularly proud of this. When you talk to them, it becomes immediately apparent that they are passionate about hydrogen research.

![Christine Heume portrait](https://www.zeiss.com/content/dam/cco/reference-master/campaigns/stories/hydrogen-juelich/portrait_christine_heume.jpg/_jcr_content/renditions/original.image_file.1920.1920.1974,297,5960,4283.file/portrait_christine_heume.jpg "Christine Heume portrait")

When we look to the future, it soon becomes clear that we must act. As a scientist, I strive to make the world a better place. That's why it feels good to be able to work on projects for a more sustainable energy industry and, in our case, to contribute to a redesign of electrolyzers.

## Hydrogen as the element of the future

Why is hydrogen important for the energy transition?

Hydrogen, which is produced with the help of renewable energies, makes it possible to reduce CO2 emissions in the industrial, transport and private sectors, for example.

Hydrogen is thus set to become a mainstay of the energy transition. Highly versatile, it offers numerous application possibilities – from a replacement gasoline to electricity storage. It is the smallest and lightest of the chemical elements. However, hydrogen plays an important role in the transformation of the energy world. The substance with the formula H2 is suitable as an alternative fuel for trucks, ships and airplanes. Hydrogen can replace crude oil and natural gas in the chemical and steel industries and act as an intermediate storage medium for renewable energies.

What challenges does the energy conversion of hydrogen entail and how is research at Forschungszentrum Jülich tackling them?

In order to establish hydrogen as an attractive energy source and storage medium, producing it needs to become more economical. However, electrolyzers are currently losing efficiency as they age. Forschungszentrum Jülich is investigating the causes and looking for solutions.

The researchers are focusing on three main areas of hydrogen research: first, the production of hydrogen. Because there are different ways to produce the "material of the future" and beacon of hope for the energy transition. The main aim is to make established processes for producing hydrogen, such as electrolysis, more cost-effective and sustainable. Second, in order to be available as an energy source at short notice, hydrogen needs to be stored safely and transported reliably. This can be done in underground storage facilities, for example in the existing natural gas grid or with the help of new technologies. Third, scientists at Forschungszentrum Jülich are working on improving the efficiency, durability and conductivity of fuel cells.

Hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) react in a fuel cell, producing water (H₂O) and electricity as a by-product.

This technology is particularly relevant for emission-free energy generation in vehicles, stationary energy sources and portable applications.

What role does ZEISS play in hydrogen research?

With X-ray microscopes such as the ZEISS Xradia 620 Versa, aging processes inside electrolyzers can be examined using the CT method without destroying them. In addition, ZEISS scanning electron microscopes such as the GeminiSEM (SEM) offer important functions for research into aging processes, allowing details to be imaged even more clearly.

ZEISS is also helping to improve the efficiency and reliability of hydrogen technologies by investing in advanced measurement techniques and software for metrology, paving the way for a clean, sustainable energy future.

----------

*   •Electrolyzer technologies (PEM, AWE, AEM, SOE) are advancing efficiency, scalability, and renewable energy integration. 
*   •Green hydrogen production costs (3.0–6.0 USD/kg) are higher than grey hydrogen (1.0–2.5 USD/kg), requiring policy support.

Abstract
--------

The global energy demand is projected to grow by 1.3% annually until 2040, driven by population growth, economic expansion, and technological advancements. Fossil fuels, which currently account for 85% of global energy consumption, are the primary contributors to greenhouse gas emissions, with over 36 billion tons of CO 2 released annually. In response, green hydrogen has emerged as a promising solution for decarbonizing energy systems, offering a sustainable alternative to fossil fuels. Produced through water electrolysis powered by renewable energy sources, green hydrogen is poised to play a pivotal role in the transition to a low-carbon economy. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the current state and future prospects of green hydrogen production, focusing on advancements in electrolyzer technologies, including Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM), Alkaline Water Electrolyzers (AWE), and Solid Oxide Electrolysis Cells (SOECs). The review highlights key innovations in electrode materials, ultrasonic field applications, machine learning (ML) optimization, and renewable energy integration, which collectively enhance the efficiency, scalability, and cost-effectiveness of hydrogen production. Additionally, the economic and environmental implications of hydrogen production methods, categorized by their “color” (green, blue, grey, brown, and black), are critically evaluated. Prominently**,** large-scale initiatives such as HyDeal España, the NEOM Green Hydrogen Project, and H2H Saltend exemplify the global momentum behind green hydrogen deployment. Despite significant progress, challenges such as high costs, material durability, and the intermittency of renewable energy sources remain barriers to widespread adoption. This review highlights the importance of continued research and development, supportive policies, and technological breakthroughs to overcome these challenges and accelerate the global transition to a hydrogen-based energy economy. By addressing these issues, green hydrogen can become a cornerstone of sustainable energy systems, contributing to climate change mitigation and energy security.

Introduction
------------

Global primary energy demand is forecasted to increase by 1.3% annually until 2040, fueled by population growth, economic development, and technological advancements [1]. As a result, fossil fuels—coal, natural gas, and oil— remain the dominant energy sources and are expected to retain their leading role until at least 2050, as illustrated in Fig. 1, Fig. 2 [2,3]. However, burning these fossil fuels releases solid particles and greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and other volatile chemicals, into the atmosphere, contributing to global climate change [4,5]. Currently, fossil fuels account for 85% of global energy consumption, with over 36 billion tons of CO 2 emitted annually to meet electricity demand. As illustrated in Fig. 2, more than 90% of these emissions are due to fossil fuel use, and this figure is expected to continue rising in the future [6].

In response to the increasing demand for cleaner energy solutions, the global green hydrogen market has shown substantial growth. In 2021, the market was valued at USD 3.2 billion and is expected to grow at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 39.5% from 2022 to 2030, as shown in Fig. 3. This significant growth highlights the increasing role of green hydrogen in the shift toward sustainable energy systems. Green hydrogen is produced via water electrolysis, powered by clean energy sources like solar and wind. The expansion of this market is supported by government policies and increasing environmental concerns regarding carbon emissions, which are expected to accelerate the transition to a hydrogen economy [7]. Green hydrogen is recognized as a crucial component in the decarbonization of various sectors, including transportation, power generation, and heavy manufacturing. Furthermore, it provides a viable solution for storing surplus sustainable energy for later use [8]. In addition to its environmental benefits, the green hydrogen sector is poised to develop new job opportunities in areas such as hydrogen production (HyPro), transportation, and storage, contributing to economic growth alongside sustainability efforts [9].

Despite its promise, green hydrogen currently lacks a standardized definition. Generally, it is characterized as hydrogen produced using clean energy sources with minimal environmental impact and carbon emissions [10]. According to Velazquez Abad [11], green hydrogen can be derived from any energy source with low carbon emissions, even if its emission intensity is not explicitly quantified [11]. Similarly, Oliveira et al. [12] defines it as hydrogen produced using energy sources that have minimal environmental and carbon impacts. A common method for generating green hydrogen is water electrolysis, which involves using electricity to separate water into hydrogen and oxygen [13]. Feedstocks for this process can include water, hydrogen sulfide, biomass, and brine. The high energy density and storage potential of hydrogen make water electrolysis an attractive approach for utilizing renewable electricity. Consequently, considerable research initiatives have been focused on improving electrolysis technologies [[14], [15], [16]].

As global demand for sustainable energy solutions increases, innovation in electrolyzer technologies becomes pivotal for improving efficiency, reducing costs, and enabling large-scale adoption [17]. This progress spans the development of robust and efficient proton exchange membrane (PEM) electrolyzers, the exploration of cost-effective anion exchange membrane (AEM) systems, and the optimization of energy-efficient solid oxide electrolysis cells (SOECs). These advancements address key challenges such as material costs, durability, and scalability, advancing the transition to a hydrogen-powered energy economy and contributing to global sustainability goals.

PEM electrolyzers, first introduced in the 1960 s [18], represent one of the most established and reliable technologies in the field of HyPro. Recognized for their high efficiency, durability, and ability to handle load fluctuations, PEM electrolyzers deliver hydrogen with exceptional purity levels of up to 99.999% [19]. However, their reliance on expensive noble metals such as platinum and iridium poses a significant cost challenge [20]. To overcome this challenge, ongoing research is focused on addressing this issue through the development of innovative catalysts and alternative manufacturing techniques to make PEM electrolyzers more cost-effective and widely accessible [21,22]. In contrast, AEM electrolyzers present a more economical alternative by utilizing non-noble metal catalysts. Despite their cost advantages, AEM electrolyzers face challenges related to material durability and long-term stability. Addressing these issues through continued advancements in component design and material optimization remains critical to unlocking their full potential [23]. Meanwhile, SOECs have emerged as a highly efficient option, particularly for industrial applications. By leveraging waste heat, SOECs achieve superior energy efficiency and contribute to significant reductions in CO 2 emissions [24]. These attributes position SOECs as a transformative technology for decarbonizing energy-intensive industries and advancing the global transition to cleaner energy systems.

Despite growing interest in green hydrogen as a sustainable energy carrier, several critical challenges continue to hinder its large-scale adoption. While numerous reviews have explored general hydrogen production (HyPro) methods, few have provided a comprehensive analysis that integrates recent advancements in electrolyzer technologies, such as novel electrode materials, ultrasonic field applications, and machine learning (ML)-based optimization. Additionally, the economic feasibility and environmental impacts of hydrogen production methods, categorized by color (green, blue, grey, etc.), remain underexplored in a structured, comparative framework.

The intermittency of renewable energy sources and the high capital and operational costs of electrolysis technologies underscore the need for innovative approaches to enhance efficiency, scalability, and affordability. This review aims to address these research gaps by evaluating the current state of electrolyzer technologies, key challenges, and emerging solutions. Specifically, it examines the integration of electrolyzers with renewable energy systems, along with their economic and environmental implications. Accordingly, the main objective could be summarized as follows:
*   •Provide a detailed assessment of the current state and advancements in electrolyzer technologies, including PEM, Alkaline Water Electrolyzers (AWE), and SOECs. 
*   •Systematically analyze the economic, technical, and infrastructural barriers to green HyPro and propose potential solutions. 
*   •Highlight recent advancements in green HyPro through electrolyzer technologies, focusing on innovations in electrode materials, ultrasonic field applications, ML optimization, and renewable energy integration. 
*   •Evaluate the economic and environmental implications of different HyPro methods categorized by color (green, blue, grey, brown, and black). 
*   •Identify key challenges in green HyPro, particularly focusing on cost reduction, material durability, and renewable energy intermittency. 
*   •Propose strategies for overcoming these challenges through technological advancements, policy support, and improved integration with renewable energy sources. 
*   •Examine real-world green hydrogen projects driving the global transition to sustainable energy. 
*   •Discuss the current status, challenges, and future directions of green HyPro. 

The review synthesizes multidisciplinary insights from material science, artificial intelligence, and energy economics, offering three key contributions:
*   •A comparative analysis of electrolyzer technologies, highlighting innovations in electrode materials, ultrasonic field applications, and ML optimization. 
*   •An evaluation of real-world hydrogen projects (e.g., HyDeal España, NEOM) to identify best practices for scaling deployment. 
*   •Policy and technology recommendations to address cost, durability, and infrastructure challenges. 

By addressing these questions, this work aims to inform stakeholders—including researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders—on pathways to accelerate the hydrogen economy’s development.

### Subjects

## Abstract

Green hydrogen represents a sustainable energy solution capable of supporting the global shift away from fossil fuels. In Algeria, with its abundant solar resources, this potential is significant. However, challenges related to water resource management and the energy cost of production limit large-scale implementation. Addressing these issues is crucial for effectively harnessing Algeria’s renewable energy potential. This study conducts an in-depth analysis leveraging advanced simulation tools like HOMER Pro to compare photovoltaic (PV) productivity and hydrogen yields in Algerian regions. The study identifies both desert regions and non-desert areas for their potential, employing innovative methods such as seawater electrolysis and wastewater utilization for sustainable water sourcing. The potential integration of hydrogen fuel cells into microgrids is also explored for enhanced energy stability and storage. The findings reveal that desert regions, such as Tamanrasset and Adrar, exhibit the highest photovoltaic electricity productivity, generating 33.5 GWh/year and 32.9 GWh/year, respectively. This translates into green hydrogen production capacities of 679 tons/year and 668 tons/year. Meanwhile, northern regions like Tlemcen and Skikda demonstrate substantial potential, producing 29 GWh/year and 26.6 GWh/year of solar electricity, which results in green hydrogen production outputs of 589 tons/year and 539 tons/year, respectively. This underscores Algeria’s ability to leverage solar energy across diverse regions. The study highlights that while desert regions exhibit high solar and hydrogen production, northern areas provide a strategic advantage due to their proximity to European markets. Algeria’s existing infrastructure supports efficient export to European markets, offering a strategic advantage in green hydrogen trade.

## Introduction

The global push for climate change mitigation demands strong laws and policies to meet international climate goals and advance the shift away from fossil fuels. Rising CO₂ levels, driven by industrial activities, highlight the urgency of adopting sustainable energy solutions, with hydrogen emerging as a critical alternative. Hydrogen’s versatility as a feedstock, fuel, and energy carrier positions it as a central component in decarbonization efforts. Currently, fossil fuels are the main source of hydrogen, yet cleaner production methods, such as electrolyzer-based hydrogen from renewables, are rapidly developing. The International Energy Agency (IEA) anticipates that the planned electrolyzer capacity for 2030 will exceed demand by 50%, underlining the momentum for green hydrogen[1](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR1 "Marzouk, O. Green hydrogen represents a cornerstone in the transition to a carbon-neutral energy landscape. It is produced through water electrolysis powered by renewable energy sources like solar and wind, making it a clean and sustainable energy solution. Its high energy storage efficiency enables applications in power generation, transportation, and industrial operations. This reduces reliance on fossil fuels and significantly cuts carbon emissions.

Technological advancements are driving reductions in production costs and improving the efficiency of electrolysis methods, reinforcing green hydrogen’s feasibility as a primary energy source. Globally, nations are competing to establish integrated infrastructures for green hydrogen. Countries like Germany, Japan, and Australia are leading with large-scale investments and innovative technologies aimed at enhancing efficiency and sustainability. This global momentum reflects a collective commitment to sustainable development and a transition to a low-carbon economy, underscoring green hydrogen’s crucial role in the future energy landscape.

Algeria, with its abundant natural resources and remarkable solar energy potential, is well-positioned to emerge as a key player in green hydrogen production. The country’s diverse geography and climate spanning sun-drenched desert areas and a temperate northern coastline offer a strong foundation for large-scale renewable energy endeavors. However, achieving this potential necessitates addressing critical factors such as the availability of solar energy, effective water resource management, and the logistical complexities of energy storage and transportation.

This study introduces a novel perspective by challenging the prevailing assumption that the Algerian desert is the optimal location for green hydrogen production. While previous studies have focused primarily on high solar radiation in the south, they have largely overlooked critical factors such as sustainable water sourcing and transportation challenges. Our research highlights the untapped potential of northern Algeria, which not only benefits from substantial solar energy potential but also offers sustainable water resources such as seawater and wastewater. Furthermore, the proximity of northern regions to the European market provides a strategic advantage for efficient hydrogen export via existing gas pipelines. This study contributes significantly to the development of a holistic and sustainable green hydrogen production strategy that balances energy potential with environmental and logistical considerations.

The Table [1](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#Tab1) summarizes recent studies related to green hydrogen from around the world:

Following an extensive review of studies on green hydrogen, we opted to undertake a detailed investigation of this topic in Algeria. This decision stems from Algeria’s recognition of hydrogen as a crucial solution to its pressing energy challenges and its commitment to transitioning toward a hydrogen-based economy. Notably, during the First International Workshop on Hydrogen: Renewable Energy Vector, held in Algiers in June 2005, key recommendations were proposed to advance this transition. These included prioritizing research, demonstration projects, education, and public outreach. Among the workshop’s outcomes were the establishment of the Algerian Hydrogen Association (2AH2) and the proposal for a collaborative Maghreb-Europe initiative. One prominent project, the Mediterranean Hydrogen Solar (MedHySol) project, focuses on developing large-scale solar hydrogen production intended for export. The project’s initial phase involves establishing a technological platform to evaluate emerging solar hydrogen production technologies, supporting breakthrough energy solutions. Subsequently, the project plans to implement cost-effective technologies for pilot projects ranging from 1 to 1000 MW[13](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR13 "Mahmah, B. et al. MedHySol: future federator project of massive production of solar hydrogen. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2008.12.068
                  
                 (2009)."). Figure [1](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#Fig1) illustrates the current distribution of Algeria’s renewable energy sources, showing solar PV as the dominant contributor at 61.7%, followed by wind at 22.7%. Biomass, solar thermal, cogeneration, and geothermal represent smaller portions.

Aligned with these efforts, Algeria has launched the HydrogeneSolareMethane (HySolThane) project under MedHySol, targeting the transportation sector. This project aims to develop Hydrogen-enriched Compressed Natural Gas (HCNG) as a fuel, starting with its implementation in buses operating in Algiers, with future plans to extend the initiative to other major cities. The overarching goal is to strategize the integration of hydrogen into Algeria’s energy economy, leveraging technologies like H2-Internal Combustion Engines in the short and medium terms. These initiatives highlight Algeria’s dedication to harnessing hydrogen as a sustainable energy alternative, aiming to establish itself as a key player in the regional energy transition[14](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR14 "Amrouche, F. et al. Toward hydrogen enriched natural gas ‘HCNG’ fuel on the algerian road, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, vol. 36, no. 6, pp. 4094–4102, Mar. (2011). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2010.07.042
                  
                ").

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-90336-1/MediaObjects/41598_2025_90336_Fig1_HTML.png)

Objectives of the updated renewable energy program in Algeria by 2035.

This study seeks to assess the feasibility and potential of green hydrogen production in different regions of Algeria, utilizing the country’s plentiful solar energy resources. By employing advanced simulation tools such as HOMER Pro, the analysis compares photovoltaic (PV) electricity productivity and green hydrogen yields in different locations, with a focus on both desert and non-desert areas. The key contributions of this study can be outlined as follows:

A comprehensive analysis demonstrating that green hydrogen production is not only more sustainable in northern Algeria but also better aligned with challenges related to water and transportation.

Highlighting the untapped potential of solar energy in northern regions, which offer high solar irradiation levels alongside sustainable water sources such as seawater and wastewater.

Proposing innovative solutions for utilizing seawater and wastewater in hydrogen production, contributing to the preservation of underground water resources.

Reviewing the economic and environmental benefits of reducing transportation and storage costs by producing hydrogen near gas pipelines and existing infrastructure.

Presenting an integrated approach that addresses the environmental and economic challenges of green hydrogen production, supporting Algeria in becoming a global leader in this field.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: “Research conducted in Algeria” Section provides an overview of previous studies and existing research on green hydrogen production in Algeria, emphasizing key accomplishments and identifying research gaps. “Water resources and potential” Section discusses Algeria’s water resources and sustainable management strategies for hydrogen production. “Solar resource” Section provides an overview of Algeria’s solar energy potential, focusing on solar irradiation distribution. “Solar electricity production in Algeria: an analysis” Section analyses solar electricity production capabilities across various regions, showcasing the country’s renewable energy potential. “Employed methodology” Section outlines the methodology, detailing the use of tools like HOMER Pro for evaluating energy system configurations and regional assessments. “Results and discussions” Section presents the results and discussion, offering a comparative analysis of photovoltaic productivity, hydrogen output, and economic and environmental impacts. Finally, “Conclusion” Section concludes with key findings, emphasizing Algeria’s strategic advantages and proposing future research directions.

## Research conducted in Algeria

Since this field is relatively new, few studies in Algeria comprehensively address these topics. A. Mraoui and S. Menia[15](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR15 "Mraoui, A. & Menia, S. Renewable electrolytic hydrogen potential in Algeria. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 44 (49), 26863–26873. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2019.08.134
                  
                 (Oct. 2019).") assessed the potential for hydrogen production through photovoltaic energy, employing detailed hourly simulations and validated models. Their findings reveal hydrogen potentials in Algeria ranging between 0.10 Nm³/m²/year and 0.14 Nm³/m²/year, particularly significant in the arid southeast and the lowlands in the northeast, although these remain largely theoretical. The northwest region, with higher population density, shows potential reaching 0.13 Nm³/m²/day. For standalone systems, the hydrogen production potential ranges from 0.07 Nm³/m²/day to 0.13 Nm³/m²/day, highlighting the need for targeted studies that compare potential resource maps with geographical, social, economic, and infrastructure data during the final selection process. DJilali Masoudi et al.[16](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR16 "Messaoudi, D. et al. Site selection methodology for the wind-powered hydrogen refueling station based on AHP-GIS in Adrar, Algeria. Energy Procedia. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.egypro.2019.04.008
                  
                 (2019)."),[17](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR17 "Messaoudi, D., Settou, N., Negrou, B. & Settou, B. GIS based multi-criteria decision making for solar hydrogen production sites selection in Algeria, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, vol. 44, no. 60, pp. 31808–31831, Dec. (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2019.10.099
                  
                ") developed a methodology to identify optimal locations for hydrogen fueling stations powered by wind energy in Adrar province. Using an integrated AHP method with GIS and applying filtering constraints, they found almost all lands in Adrar promising for wind hydrogen production. Out of 15 stations, 4 were chosen for upgrading, with 3 in high suitability zones and 1 In a moderately suitable zone, it has been observed that most fuel stations are situated in areas unsuitable for wind turbine installations. Gougui et al.[18](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR18 "Gougui, A., Djafour, A., Danoune, M. B. & Khelfaoui, N. Field experience study and evaluation for hydrogen production through a photovoltaic system in Ouargla region, Algeria. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2019.11.188
                  
                 (2020).") examined hydrogen production through a photovoltaic-electrolyzer system in the Ouargla region. Similarly, Mouloud Baik[19](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR19 "Baik, M., Hammoudi, M., Salhi, Y. & Kirati, S. K. Hydrogen production by hybrid system and its conversion by fuel cell in Algeria; Djanet. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 43 (6), 3466–3474. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.11.074
                  
                 (Feb. 2018).") explored a hybrid power generation station in the Gant area, integrating solar and wind energy to investigate renewable sources for hydrogen generation. Khelfaoui et al.[20](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR20 "Khelfaoui, N. et al. Experimental investigation of solar hydrogen production PV/PEM electrolyser performance in the Algerian Sahara regions, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, vol. 46, no. 59, pp. 30524–30538, Aug. (2021). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2020.11.193
                  
                ") conducted an experimental analysis of PV/PEM systems for solar hydrogen production in the desert regions of Algeria. In another study, Lemya Bentoumi[21](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR21 "Bentoumi, L., Miles, A. & Korei, Z. Green hydrogen production by integrating a solar power plant with a combined cycle in the desert climate of Algeria. Sol Energy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2024.112311
                  
                 (2024).") assessed the feasibility of hydrogen production by utilizing a CSP plant integrated with SRC and ORC cycles in the Illizi and Tindouf desert areas. The analysis, which incorporated AE, PEME, and SOE electrolyzers, highlighted Illizi as the most favorable location, with a power output exceeding 60 kW and an energy efficiency of 24.9% recorded in September. The production rates of hydrogen were highest using SOE electrolyzers (219 Nm³), followed by PEME (185 Nm³), and AE (148 Nm³). Additionally, Samia Rahmouni et al.[22](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR22 "Rahmouni, S., Negrou, B., Settou, N., Dominguez, J. & Gouareh, A. Prospects of hydrogen production potential from renewable resources in Algeria. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2016.07.214
                  
                 (2017).") estimated Algeria’s hydrogen production potential through water electrolysis powered by solar and wind energy. Their findings indicated significant wind energy potential in Laghouat (Hassi R’Mel area) and Adrar, with energy outputs of 1074.88 and 915.09 GWh/km²/year, respectively, leading to hydrogen production estimates of 18,447.6 and 15,705.3 tons/km²/year. For solar energy, the highest potentials were observed in Tamanrasset and El Tarf, with corresponding hydrogen production capacities of 6,327.19 and 4,437.14 tons/km²/year. These studies collectively demonstrate that renewable energy systems achieve greater efficiency in Algeria’s desert regions.

Upon thorough examination of these studies, it becomes evident that they predominantly focus on one critical aspect of green hydrogen production: the energy source. Each study unanimously concluded that the desert regions of Algeria are ideal for establishing green hydrogen production plants due to their high efficiency in harnessing photovoltaic and wind renewable energy. However, an equally important aspect has been largely overlooked: the water source required for the hydrogen production process.

Neither A. Mraoui[15](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR15 "Mraoui, A. & Menia, S. Renewable electrolytic hydrogen potential in Algeria. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 44 (49), 26863–26873. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2019.08.134
                  
                 (Oct. 2019).") nor DJilali Masoudi[16](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR16 "Messaoudi, D. et al. Site selection methodology for the wind-powered hydrogen refueling station based on AHP-GIS in Adrar, Algeria. Energy Procedia. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.egypro.2019.04.008
                  
                 (2019)."),[17](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR17 "Messaoudi, D., Settou, N., Negrou, B. & Settou, B. GIS based multi-criteria decision making for solar hydrogen production sites selection in Algeria, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, vol. 44, no. 60, pp. 31808–31831, Dec. (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2019.10.099
                  
                ") addressed the water source issue in their research. Although the renewable energy yield in these desert areas is promising, it alone is insufficient for comprehensive green hydrogen production. The pressing question remains: where will we source the necessary water in these arid regions? Samia Rahmouni[22](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR22 "Rahmouni, S., Negrou, B., Settou, N., Dominguez, J. & Gouareh, A. Prospects of hydrogen production potential from renewable resources in Algeria. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2016.07.214
                  
                 (2017).") proposed using groundwater, but this solution poses significant risks to Algeria’s non-renewable water resources. Furthermore, the studies did not consider the potential impact of high temperatures on hydrogen, nor did they address the challenges related to its storage and transportation.

The proposed study aims to conduct a deep analytical study of all the challenges associated with green hydrogen production. This involves not only identifying sites with high renewable energy potential but also guaranteeing access to a reliable and sustainable water supply. The main objective is to avoid depleting vital underground water resources, such as groundwater, and to explore innovative methods to leverage hydrogen production for secondary goals. This holistic approach will contribute to sustainable development and position Algeria as a leading nation in green hydrogen production.

## Water resources and potential

Algeria boasts extensive hydraulic groundwater reserves alongside significant renewable energy potential as shown in Fig. [2](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#Fig2)[17](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR17 "Messaoudi, D., Settou, N., Negrou, B. & Settou, B. GIS based multi-criteria decision making for solar hydrogen production sites selection in Algeria, Int. J. Hydrogen Energy, vol. 44, no. 60, pp. 31808–31831, Dec. (2019). 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2019.10.099
                  
                "), positioning it to pursue large-scale renewable hydrogen production through partnerships with international collaborators[13](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR13 "Mahmah, B. et al. MedHySol: future federator project of massive production of solar hydrogen. Int. J. Hydrogen Energy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2008.12.068
                  
                 (2009)."). Spanning a landmass of 2.4 million km², Algeria is the largest nation in North Africa. Despite 87% of its territory being arid desert with limited rainfall, it holds substantial fossil groundwater resources. In contrast, the northern region benefits from a Mediterranean climate, which sustains both surface and renewable underground water sources.

The Tell region, encompassing approximately 7% of Algeria’s total area, accounts for 90% of its surface water reserves. Conversely, the southern Sahara primarily depends on fossil groundwater with minimal natural recharge. Algeria has identified six principal aquifers, with the majority of water resources concentrated in two major confined systems: the Terminal Complex (CT) and the Continental Interlayer (CI). These systems are jointly managed with neighboring Libya and Tunisia, forming the transboundary Septentrional Saharan Aquifer System (SSAS).

Annual rainfall in Algeria is estimated at 100 billion m³, with approximately 80% lost to atmospheric evaporation. The country’s total water resources are evaluated at 19.1 billion m³ annually, including 12.4 billion m³ from surface sources and 6.7 billion m³ from underground reserves. However, only 6 billion m³ are effectively harnessed through dam reservoirs, with current utilization at roughly 4 billion m³ across 110 operational dams. In the Sahara, groundwater reserves are estimated at 5.1 billion m³, with 1.6 billion m³ already exploited at an 80% capacity, mainly through wells and boreholes[23](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR23 "Boudghene Stambouli, A. et al. Trends and challenges of sustainable energy and water research in North Africa: Sahara solar breeder concerns at the intersection of energy/water. Renew. Sustain. Energy Rev. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rser.2013.11.042
                  
                 (2014)."),[24](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR24 "Drouiche, N., Ghaffour, N., Naceur, M. W., Lounici, H. & Drouiche, M. Towards sustainable water management in Algeria. Desalin. Water Treat. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/19443994.2012.719477
                  
                 (2012).").

## Solar resource

Algeria ranks among the top countries globally in terms of solar irradiation, making it exceptionally suited for solar energy development. Annual solar irradiation averages range from 1,700 kWh/m² in the northern regions to over 2,200 kWh/m² in the southern desert areas. With around 3,000 h of sunshine annually, Algeria possesses a vast and untapped solar energy potential, positioning it as a leading candidate for large-scale solar energy projects. Leveraging this resource could play a critical role in advancing the country’s renewable energy targets, decreasing reliance on fossil fuels, and enabling green hydrogen production, thus driving Algeria’s energy transition forward. ## Solar electricity production in Algeria: an analysis

Algeria holds remarkable potential for solar electricity generation, utilizing mono-crystalline photovoltaic panels with a peak power rating of 250 W and an efficiency of 15.28%. Analysis of the spatial distribution of solar power across the country indicates a substantial production capacity, ranging from 250 to 370 GWh/km² annually. The Tamenrasset region emerges as the top performer, with an annual solar electricity potential of 368.7 GWh/km², followed by the Adrar region at 328.9 GWh/km². Bechar, situated in the extreme west, ranks third with an average annual output of 321.1 GWh/km². Figure [4](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#Fig4) illustrates the projected solar electricity production across various regions, highlighting geographical and climatic influences on generation capacity.

These findings emphasize Algeria’s exceptional solar energy potential, particularly in the southern regions. This makes large-scale hydrogen production powered by solar energy a compelling opportunity, underscoring Algeria’s strategic position to drive renewable energy advancements[25](/articles/s41598-025-90336-1#ref-CR25 "Kichene, M. et al. Performance Investigation of a Large-Scale Grid-Tied PV Plant under High Plateau Climate Conditions: Case Study Ain El-Melh, Algeria, J. Eur. des Syst. Autom., vol. 56, no. 3, pp. 483–492, Jun. (2023).

## Employed methodology

The study begins with the proposal for establishing a 20-megawatt solar power station in the Ain Salah region of Algeria, which is already operational and providing energy to approximately 100 households. This solar station generates around 1,400 kilowatt-hours of electricity per day, ensuring a reliable supply of clean energy to the local community. Surplus energy generated during peak solar hours is directed to an electrolyzer for the production of green hydrogen. The hydrogen is then stored in pressurized tanks, allowing it to serve as a backup energy source during periods of low solar availability. This dynamic approach ensures continuous energy supply and minimizes reliance on non-renewable energy sources.

The system employs a real-time energy management strategy to optimize resource allocation. During periods of high solar energy generation, priority is given to meeting local energy demand. Any surplus energy is then routed to the electrolyzer for hydrogen production. The HOMER software was utilized to simulate and optimize the system’s operation, ensuring efficient integration of solar energy, hydrogen production, and storage components. This methodology supports the development of a sustainable and reliable energy system for Algeria’s unique climate conditions.
